title: Equations of Motion
subtitle: Three equations of motion for Class 11 Physics — derivation, graphical proof and solved examples

blocks.heading: The Idea
blocks.body:
When acceleration is uniform (constant), three elegant equations connect the five quantities: initial velocity u, final velocity v, acceleration a, displacement s, and time t. Each equation uses four of the five — pick the one that has the quantity you want and excludes the one you don't know.
These equations are derived purely from the definitions of velocity and acceleration — no new physics is needed, just algebra.

blocks.heading: The Formula
blocks.body:
The three kinematic equations for uniform acceleration (constant a):
A fourth useful relation (average velocity × time):
Symbols:
u — initial velocity (m/s)
v — final velocity (m/s)
a — uniform acceleration (m/s²)
s — displacement (m)
t — time elapsed (s)
Validity: these equations hold only when acceleration is constant. For variable acceleration, integration is needed.
Free fall: replace a with g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2 (downward). Taking downward as positive: v = u + gt, h = ut + \frac{1}{2}gt^2, etc.

blocks.heading: Real-World Example
blocks.body: An aircraft needs a minimum speed of 80 m/s to take off. Its engines give it a constant acceleration of 4 m/s². Using v^2 = u^2 + 2as with u = 0: 80^2 = 0 + 2(4)s, so s = 6400/8 = 800 \text{ m}. The runway must be at least 800 m long — airport designers use exactly this calculation.

blocks.heading: Try It At Home
blocks.body: Drop a small ball from a known height (say 1 m). Time how long it takes to hit the floor. Use h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2 to estimate g. Repeat from 2 m. Do you get roughly 9.8 m/s² each time? (Use a phone stopwatch — or two phones for better accuracy.)

blocks.heading: What If…
blocks.body: What if a = 0 in equation (2)? You get s = ut — constant velocity. What if u = 0 too? Then s = \frac{1}{2}at^2 — the classic free-fall result. The equations collapse to simpler forms at extreme values.

quiz.question: A stone is thrown vertically upward at 20 m/s. Taking g = 10 m/s² downward, how high does it go?
quiz.explanation: At the highest point v = 0. Using v² = u² − 2gh: 0 = 400 − 2(10)h → h = 400/20 = 20 m.
